title: Two quadlets with the same filename in different directories will omit one of them
body:
### Issue Description

The Quadlet binary will recursively read all directory in the places where it expect quadlets file; however if the quadlet has not specified a `ServiceName=` it will use the filename, no matter where it is located.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Let's have two quadlets as followed (must be named the same).
```bash
~/.config/containers/systemd
├── bar.container
└── dummy
    └── bar.container
```
2. Running `quadlet --user --dryrun` will only a single quadlet
```bash
$: /usr/libexec/podman/quadlet --user -dryrun
quadlet-generator[31652]: Loading source unit file /home/axel7083/.config/containers/systemd/bar.container
---bar.service---
[...]
```


### Describe the results you received

I only have one Quadlet generated 

### Describe the results you expected

I would expect to see two quadlets.

### podman info output

```yaml
Using main branch
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

N/A

### Additional information

The problem is a bit complex, the main issue is how the _recursivity_ is made, the quadlet binary uses a first method `GetUnitDirs`

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/34166fc004b8507291c60082ce9ba6eadc955ca6/pkg/systemd/quadlet/unitdirs.go#L42

However this method is a bit misleading (IMHO) it does not only return the directories, but it does a recursive in the directories, so for the example above, running the debugger showed me that this method return the following

```json
[
    "/run/user/1000/containers/systemd",
    "/home/axel7083/.config/containers/systemd",
    "/home/axel7083/.config/containers/systemd/dummy", <-- this is weird to have it here
    "/etc/containers/systemd/users",
    "/etc/containers/systemd/users/1000"
]
```

Then in `loadUnitsFromDir`, we use `ReadDir` which only return the entries for the current directory without doing any recursivity. 

⚠️ this is a problem, because IMHO the quadlet binary should prefix the nested directories in the service name to avoid the problem of duplicate service name.

Taking our example above again, I would expect two service names
- `bar.service`
- `dummy-bar.service`

But to be able to compute the relative path, you need to walk from the config directory, and not the sub directories themself, so they might need to have a shift, where `GetUnitDirs` should only return the parent directory where unit files can be, and `loadUnitsFromDir` explore those recursively

Linked to https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28118

I made https://github.com/axel7083/podman/tree/draft/quadlet/service-name-application-prefix as a POC to explore how the nested directories could be used to prefix the service name, this idea could be useful for https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28117#issuecomment-4040695507

comments.body:
This is working as designed to so I remove the bug label and add the feature.

The multiple reclusive directory thing added to quadlet was only about file organization not namespacing https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/413552e10e35802c79c91162ea5ad69bdd8fea47 https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18032

In general there are different use cases, by default well just like systemd we have a flat namespace where we cannot have conflicts so it was always the user responsibility do deploy quadlets with service names which do not cause conflicts.
In particular a quadlet service name may very well be able to overwrite a regular non quadlet service. 
So as admin of the system would need to make sure you name the quadlet right just like with normal systemd units so I think most are fine with that when they deploy custom units for themselves.

On the other hand there are users who just like to copy some existing quadlet files from somewhere or use podman quadlet install like described in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28117 and then do nothing about namespacing. So this use case of sharing stuff would become quite hard as they could only ever be one nginx.container which of course is not realistic, so in this case having a directory as project name makes total sense and would also help when looking for unit statuses with systemd as they would all have the same name prefix.


> this is a problem, because IMHO the quadlet binary should prefix the nested directories in the service name to avoid the problem of duplicate service name.

While this is a reasonable fix to help with the namespace conflicts it is major breaking change for anyone depending on the current generated service names.
It also means if file a has Dependencies on other services in the same directory with there normal flat service name they all just break. So to avoid this quadlet would need to ensure to rewrite all these service dependencies correctly which I think would get somewhat complicated.

The other obvious issue is a filename cannot be longer than 255 chars, if we have long name and/or deep nested structures that limit can be easily hit, so what should we do about that. The service name generation logic must be consistent for predicable service names so users can interact with the units so we cannot just suddenly trim the path, can we?


---

In general while we can make breaking changes with podman 6 or later major versions I rather not break existing things like that when there is good way to address the problem. It would break basically anyone who currently uses subdirectories.

I guess the only comprise I could think of is have podman quadlet install rewrite the files to include the ServiceName= option which the naming schema you suggest together with doing the in directory installation discussed in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28117#issuecomment-4040695507. That should solve the second use case

As @Luap99 stated, the reason for this behaviour is that Quadlet is a systemd generator and as such tries to follow similar patterns as much as possible. One such pattern is the flat namespace. Can it be changed? Sure. Will it open a new can of warms? Most likely. Personally, I don't think it's worth it.

>  One such pattern is the flat namespace

Thanks @Luap99 and @ygalblum really appreciate the detailed explanation, I'm not familiar enough with systemd, and now I understand better the flat namespace logic, I'm closing it for now as this would be a strong breaking change and would lead to many many problems!

For the use case describe in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28117 I think we could argue that a good practice would be to specify the ServiceName to avoid conflicts
